titlestmt.titleproper: Guide to the William Henry Zuylestein, Earl of Rochford, Papers
Rochford (William Henry Zuylestein, Earl of) papers
titlestmt.author: by Sandra Markham

descriptivenote.p: Finding aid description and metadata in Archives at Yale are licensed under a CC0 1.0 Universal (CC0 1.0) Public Domain Dedication. This means that anyone may copy, modify, and distribute this descriptive metadata without restriction or permission. The CC0 license is explained in full on the Creative Commons website: CC0 1.0 Universal (CC0 1.0) Public Domain Dedication. More information is available on our Help page.

maintenanceevent.eventdescription: This finding aid was produced using ArchivesSpace on Tuesday March 5, 2024 at 02:00

did.unittitle: William Henry Zuylestein, Earl of Rochford, papers
did.abstract: The William Henry Zuylestein, Earl of Rochford, Papers contain correspondence and documents concerning his government service and personal affairs. The more than forty letters are primarily from diplomatic colleagues including George Cressner in Bonn, Sir John Goodricke in Stockholm, Fulke Greville in Munich, Sir Robert Gunning in Copenhagen, Sir William Hamilton in Naples, William Petty, Marquis of Lansdowne, in Paris, David Murray, Earl of Mansfield, in Vienna, Andrew Mitchell in Berlin, William Norton in Bern, Robert Walpole in Madrid, Ralph Woodford in Hamburg, James Wright in Venice, Thomas Wroughton in Warsaw, and Joseph Dover, Baron Yorke, in The Hague. There are two reports from the Battle of Tiruvannamalai in South India, on September 25-27, 1767, and other letters concerning some of his real estate transactions. A small group of Other Papers include some writings, accounts of British government expenses (1769-1777), and an ancestral document in Dutch from 1688 signed by King William III, then the Prince of Orange.

persname.part: Rochford, William Henry Nassau de Zuylestein, Earl of, 1717-1781

accessrestrict.head: Conditions Governing Access
accessrestrict.p: This collection is open for research.

acqinfo.head: Immediate Source of Acquisition
acqinfo.p: W. S. Lewis purchased the papers from G. H. Last in 1933. Bequest of Wilmarth Sheldon Lewis (Yale 1918), 1979.

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: Organized into two series: I. Correspondence, 1766-1780. II. Other Papers, 1688-1823.

bioghist.head: Biographical / Historical
bioghist.p: William Henry Nassau de Zuylestein, 4th Earl of Rochford (1717-1781) was a British diplomat of Anglo-Dutch descent, the son of Frederick Nassau van Zuylestein, 3rd Earl of Rochford, and his wife Elizabeth Savage. He was born in St Osyth, Essex, attended Eton College (1725-1732) and the Collège de Genève, where he became fluent in French; he took the title as the 4th Earl of Rochford when his father died in 1738. After various court appointments in England, Rochford held ambassadorial posts in Turin (1749-1755), Madrid (1763-1766) and Paris (1766-1768), and served as Secretary of State in both the Northern (1768-1770) and Southern (1770-1775) departments. He retired from service in 1775, and died at St Osyth on September 29, 1781.

prefercite.head: Preferred Citation
prefercite.p: William Henry Zuylestein, Earl of Rochford, Papers. The Lewis Walpole Library, Yale University.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: The William Henry Zuylestein, Earl of Rochford, Papers contain correspondence and documents concerning his government service and personal affairs. The more than forty letters are primarily from diplomatic colleagues including George Cressner in Bonn, Sir John Goodricke in Stockholm, Fulke Greville in Munich, Sir Robert Gunning in Copenhagen, Sir William Hamilton in Naples, William Petty, Marquis of Lansdowne in Paris, David Murray, Earl of Lansdowne in Vienna, Andrew Mitchell in Berlin, William Norton in Bern, Robert Walpole in Madrid, Ralph Woodford in Hamburg, James Wright in Venice, Thomas Wroughton in Warsaw, and Joseph Dover, Baron Yorke, in The Hague. There are two reports from the Battle of Tiruvannamalai in South India, on September 25-27, 1767, and other letters concerning some of his real estate transactions. A small group of Other Papers include some writings, accounts of British government expenses (1769-1777), and an ancestral document in Dutch from 1688 signed by King William III, then the Prince of Orange.

userestrict.head: Conditions Governing Use
userestrict.p: The William Henry Zuylestein, Earl of Rochford, Papers is the physical property of the Lewis Walpole Library, Yale University. Literary rights, including copyright, belong to the authors or their legal heirs and assigns. For further information, consult the W. S. Lewis Librarian/Executive Director.

persname.part: Rochford, William Henry Nassau de Zuylestein, Earl of, 1717-1781

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: The letters in the series are primarily those received by Rochford from his diplomatic colleagues, but in the folder for William Petty, Marquis of Lansdowne there are also drafts of Rochford's replies. Many of the letters simply congratulations to him on overseas appointments, notably as ambassador to Paris in 1766, or sending news and intelligence, sometimes with parts in cypher.

The folder for William Hamilton holds three letters from him; his letter of February 16, 1768, sent from Rome, asks Rochford to review the first volume of his Antiquités Etrusques, Grecques et Romaines Tireés du cabinet de M. Hamilton , which he has sent by packet, and to send it right away to the King so he would have it before the cargo of books he has shipped to London arrives. Hamilton describes the author, Pierre d'Hancarville, as a "French man of genious whom I found in distress at Naples" and writes that the work is "being executed in a new manner." The four-volume work carries a publication date of 1766 and 1767 though his 1768 letter reveals that: "Another volume will be delivered in May next, and two others are almost ready for the Press."

Other non-diplomatic letters include two copies of letters sent from the engineers D. P. Desmaretz and Andres Frazer to William Petty Fitzmaurice, Marquess of Lansdowne, then the Earl of Shelburne and Secretary of State for the Southern Department, giving their analyses of the navigation issues in the English Channel at Dunkirk. and one from Bishop Robert Lowth, who sought Rochford's support for Benjamin Kennicott's work in Paris on an international collation of Hebrew manuscripts.

Rochford's real estate dealings are the focus of a letter from lawyer Peter Holford of Westonbirt, who offers to assist with the transaction regarding Rochford's payoff of the mortgage of the Essex estate. Another land transaction is documented in the set of letters from the Ipswich lawyer Samuel Kilderbee, Lincoln's Inn lawyer Daniel Macnamara, and Richard Rigby.

did.unittitle: Desmaretz, J. P., and Andrew Frazer, -1792
Grafton, Augustus Henry Fitzroy, Duke of, 1735-1811

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Also present are three letters from Rochford to Petty; he was at the time Earl of Shelburne.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Also present is a copy of Rochford's reply to the lawyer.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: A draft of a letter from the Earl of Rochford's secretary regarding the Nassau family's armorial bearings.

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: Series II is organized into two subseries: Financial and Legal Documents, and Essays, Poems, and Other Writings.

did.unittitle: An account of the annual cost of the civil government and the royal family, 1769-1777

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: A document titled "An Accompt of the Payments for His Majesty's Civil Government and for Support and Maintenance of His Household and Royal Family from the 5th Janry 1769 to the 5th Janry 1777 distingiushing [sic] each Year and the several Heads of Expences."

did.unittitle: An account of the funds that would have been applicable to the expenses of his Majesty's civil government

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: A document titled "An Account shewing the Amount of the Monies that would have been applicable to the Expences of His Majesty's Civil Government if the Hereditary and Temporary Revenues, and so much as was granted to His late Majesty out of the Aggregate Fund and Duty arising from Wine Licences had been granted to His present Majesty from His Accession to 5 Janry 1777 The Amount of the Annuity received by His Majesty in lieu of those Revenues; And the Advantage to the Public after deducting as well the said Annuity as the Money granted by Parliament to pay off the Civil List Debt on 5th Janry 1769."

did.unittitle: Indenture between John Williamson and Peter Lavernee

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Receipt to Earl of Rochford for £1500 consolidated in 4 per cents.

did.unittitle: "An Address to the Gentlemen, Clergy, & Freeholders of the County of Suffolk"

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Incomplete and in an unidentified hand.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Two arithmetic problems, each written on its own sheet, one entitled "Single rule of three" and the other "Double rule of three"; in an unidentified hand.

did.unittitle: "The King's Stile to a Duke, an Arch Bishop, a Marquess"

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Salutations for a king addressing duke, an archbishop, and a marquess with Latin translations. On recto: "Way bill" for moving packets.

did.unittitle: "Verses to be added to Mr. Clarke's character of the Spanish Court"

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Work referred to is Edward Clarke, Letters Concerning the Spanish Nation: Written at Madrid during the years 1760 and 1761 (London, 1763).

did.unittitle: Document in Dutch signed by Wilhelm Hendrick, Prince of Orange, 1650-1702

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Document includes the name "Wilhem Nassau" in two places, likely William Henry van Nassau van Zuylesteyn, Earl of Rochford, 1645-1709.

did.unittitle: Estimate for repairing or rebuilding Dedham parsonage, prepared by M. G. Thompson
